title: About
text: About this blog

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text: This project will take published data from Seattle Police Crime reports. We will be creating a pipeline for this data to be available in a database, and then map out locations for common areas of activity.

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
The Tools

Socrata API — API used by government agencies to publish crime data for many different cities across the country
Python and SQL — For the pipeline we will be using Python to transfer into a PostgreSQL
Polars — A DataFrame library used for the transformation

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
Why These Tools?
Scorata API is one of the best options for crime logs and statistics, as well as being a significantly more efficient method to obtaining crime data. Polars is used within Python as much more efficient for loading larger data sets than Pandas.

title: Building a Pipeline for Crime Data - Introduction
text:
ETL Process

Run Socrata API to extract the data from the web.
Transform data to be clean and ready for use.
Validate processes.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is pretty standard procedure for data engineering. Breaking it down it is sthe step by step process of getting this data into a database for further use.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Extract
Right of the bat, Socrata API becomes one of the greatest assets to extracting Seattle’s crime data. As mentioned before Socrata is used by government agencies to publish crime data. It will scrape Seattle’s public records on the Police department’s website. Because it contains such vast data, I limited the results to just 50,000 results as too much at once can crash less powerful computers. Finished this process by loading it into a Polars dataframe.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Transform
Two files are attached to this process (the obvious “transform.py”, as well as validate.py). The first begins by doing a lot of clean up. This amounts to removing duplicates and null/NA type values. It splits our time values into individual unique columns, as well as properly assigning datatypes for latitude and longitude columns. This is vital for better data manipulation which will come in to play with GIS mapping later on. The validate file does exactly what its title would imply; verifies expected columns and their logic.

title: Extract, Load, and Transform - Getting everything I need in one place.
text:
Load
Connecting through the env file, we find the path for the data. Here we get to create the table in a SQL query that will let us have fun later. SQLAlchemy allows to both write the query for the table and the engine to execute.

title: Bugs, Pipeline, and Dashboard
text: This was the part that took the longest, as many adjustments had to be made for the formatting of the pipeline to go into Polars. Additionally, I had to spend some time setting up Docker to be able to run PostgreSQL after fixing the pipeline’s issues. It ran fine afterwards and I was able to get what I needed. Witho the amount of data that Seattle contains, it’s worth considering using something like MangoDB or DuckDB to run ALL of the data. For time’s sake, I’m leaving it as is, but this will be worth looking into later on for optimization purposes.

title: Bugs, Pipeline, and Dashboard
text:
Dashboard
After wrapping up with those fixes, I began work on getting a dashboard with the basic crime analytics needed. Even though I’m still not 100% familiar with navigating Metabase’s system and UI, I found that producing general analyitcs for my data was relatively simple. For example, we almost immediately visualize property crime as the most common category. We have a map with all the points automatically highlighted at the top as well. This is like a small sample of what we’ll do in our GIS work.

objectID: posts/04-GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package/index.html
href: posts/04-GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package/index.html
title: GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package
text: This is the beginning of the end, as well as learning a lot of previously unfamiliar tools. GeoPandas is the most commonly used package in Python for GIS work. The formatting is very similar to (believe it or not) Pandas, and loading in files and performing basic functions is about the same. I had to go back and update in the load file, and added a geometry column based on the latitude and longitude. This is essential, along with getting a coordinate. 4326 is a standard identifier for maps, something that doesn’t need to be explained to GIS experts, but I had to write this in load.py as well as write this in the crs before beginning my work.

objectID: posts/04-GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package/index.html#pysal
href: posts/04-GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package/index.html#pysal
title: GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package
text:
PySAL
PySAL is a more niche package for construction and ineractive editing of spatial weights matrices and graphs. Documentation and videos definitely are out there, but I had to do more digging to be able to get a good start. I begin by choosing the method to measure the neighborhoods to see significance. I used “Queen Contiguity” which compares all surrounding neighborhoods to one another. Here I established a cold/hot map to indicate the hotspots on the map beforehnad. PySAL along with the help of the Folium and Leaflet packages help create a more detailed hotspot map.

title: GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package
text:
Creating hotmaps: My Python Package
The process of setup is a bit verbose and lengthy. Which is what gave me the idea of hotmaps. Not only does it make a lot of this work easier to do, I think this would be great to pitch it’s use to make the work easier as well as more appealing for use. Looking at a lot of government websites with any kind of GIS mapping, they aren’t particularly good, and I think not only can more be done in those types of settings, but it can be done mor easily while retaining quality.
Main Package features: - Loading my databse - Time Filter - Build Weights - Visualize with the maps

title: GeoPandas, PySAL, and Creating Hotmaps Package
text: Last part of this project had me learning a lot of previously unfamiliar tools. GeoPandas is the most commonly used package in Python for GIS work. The formatting is very similar to (believe it or not) Pandas, and loading in files and performing basic functions is about the same. I had to go back and update in the load file, and added a geometry column based on the latitude and longitude. This is essential, along with getting a coordinate. 4326 is a standard identifier for maps, something that doesn’t need to be explained to GIS experts, but I had to write this in load.py as well as write this in the crs before beginning my work.

text:
PySAL
PySAL is a more niche package for construction and ineractive editing of spatial weights matrices and graphs. Documentation and videos definitely are out there, but I had to do more digging to be able to get a good start. I begin by choosing the method to measure the neighborhoods to see significance. I used “Queen Contiguity” which compares all surrounding neighborhoods to one another. Here I established a cold/hot map to indicate the hotspots on the map beforehnad. PySAL along with the help of the Folium and Leaflet packages help create a more detailed hotspot map.
